coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of Ag nanoparticles/carbon dots/TiO2 nanotube arrays composite photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Xiaohui
Wang, Chunlai
Ding, Sheng
Yang, Kun
Tian, Feng
Li, Fan
coredata.dc:description: A silver nanoparticle (Ag NP)/carbon dot (CD)/TiO2 nanotube array (TNTA) composite with enhanced photocatalytic activity was solvothermally synthesized by a simple one-step method. TNTA comprised vertically arranged and highly ordered TiO2 nanotubes decorated by CDs and Ag NPs, with the amount of TNTA-supported Ag depending on the precursor (AgNO3) loading. Methylene blue photodegradation tests under visible and UV light were used to test the photocatalytic activity of the above composite and its recyclability, revealing that Ag/CDs/TNTA exhibited high durability and was more active than its individual components or two-component combinations. This behavior was ascribed to the suppressed recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the enhanced visible light absorption due to the surface plasmon resonance effect of Ag NPs and the photoelectrical properties of CDs. Thus, the developed photocatalyst was concluded to hold great promise for wastewater treatment and other environmental remediation applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ag nanoparticles
Carbon dots (CDs)
TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNTA)
Photocatalytic activity
Surface plasmon resonance (SPR)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of TNTA, CDs/TNTA, and Ag/CDs/TNTA.
Raman spectra of TNTA, CDs/TNTA, and Ag/CDs/TNTA.
SEM images of (a, b) TNTA and (d, e) CDs/TNTA. EDX spectra of (c) TNTA and (f) CDs/TNTA.
SEM images of (a, b) 1-Ag/CDs/TNTA, (c, d) 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA, and (e, f) 3-Ag/CDs/TNTA.
(a) STEM image, (b–e) EDX mappings of Ti, O, C and Ag, and (f) EDX spectrum of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA.
(a) Full survey, (b) Ag 3d, (c) C 1s, (d) O 1s, and (e) Ti 2p spectra of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA.

Photocatalytic activity (under UV light) and recyclability of different catalysts. (a) MB degradation kinetics of different catalysts under UV light. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA acquired during MB degradation. (c) MB degradation kinetics of different catalysts under UV light. (d) Results of three repeated 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA-catalyzed MB degradation experiments.
(a) Photocatalytic activity (under visible light) and (b) linear fittings of the pseudo-first-order kinetics model for MB degradation over different photocatalysts under visible light.
Diffuse reflectance spectra of TNTA, CDs/TNTA, and Ag/CDs/TNTA.
PL spectra of different samples (excitation at 320 nm).

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of Ag nanoparticles/carbon dots/TiO2 nanotube arrays composite photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medical Support Technology, Academy of Military Sciences, Tianjin, 300161, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Medical Support Technology, Academy of Military Sciences, Tianjin, 300161, People’s Republic of China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of National Nuclear Biological and Chemical Disaster Protection, Research Institution of Chemical Defense, Beijing, 100191, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of National Nuclear Biological and Chemical Disaster Protection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of National Nuclear Biological and Chemical Disaster Protection, Research Institution of Chemical Defense, Beijing, 100191, People’s Republic of China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Co-first author with the same contribution to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A silver nanoparticle (Ag NP)/carbon dot (CD)/TiO2 nanotube array (TNTA) composite with enhanced photocatalytic activity was solvothermally synthesized by a simple one-step method. TNTA comprised vertically arranged and highly ordered TiO2 nanotubes decorated by CDs and Ag NPs, with the amount of TNTA-supported Ag depending on the precursor (AgNO3) loading. Methylene blue photodegradation tests under visible and UV light were used to test the photocatalytic activity of the above composite and its recyclability, revealing that Ag/CDs/TNTA exhibited high durability and was more active than its individual components or two-component combinations. This behavior was ascribed to the suppressed recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the enhanced visible light absorption due to the surface plasmon resonance effect of Ag NPs and the photoelectrical properties of CDs. Thus, the developed photocatalyst was concluded to hold great promise for wastewater treatment and other environmental remediation applications.

ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 nanotubes attract much attention due to their high photocatalytic activity, relatively low cost, large surface area, strong adsorption capacity, and easily controllable diameter [1–4] and can be prepared in a number of ways, e.g., by template-assisted [5,6], hydrothermal [7,8], and electrochemical anodization [9,10] techniques. In particular, electrochemical anodization refers to the preparation of highly ordered TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNTA) with a vertical nanotube orientation by Ti film anodization [11]. TNTA have an ordered array structure with an adjustable pore size for secure attachment to the Ti film and feature an enhanced light absorption ability due to the inhibitory effect of multiple reflections from nanotube walls on the escape of photons from the nanotubes. Compared with other one-dimensional TiO2 nanomaterials, TNTA exhibit higher corrosion resistance, mechanical stability, and recyclability [12,13] but suffer from a limited light absorption range and an ineffective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. These challenges have been addressed in many ways, e.g., by sensitization with quantum dots [14,15], noble metal deposition [16], doping with metals [17,18] or non-metals [19–21], and coupling with other semiconductors [22].

The recently discovered carbon dots (CDs) display excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and good photoelectrical performance, e.g., good electron transport and energy storage capability as well as extensive light absorption and photoluminescence (PL) [23–25]. Consequently, the combination of TiO2 with CDs promotes both electron-hole pair separation and light absorption [26–28]. Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) are cheaper than other noble metal NPs and exhibit a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect that can be used to extend the light absorption of photocatalysts to longer wavelengths [29]. In our previous study, a CD/Ag NP/P25 TiO2 nanocomposite was shown to exhibit an enhanced light absorption capacity and charge transfer ability due to the photoelectrical properties and the SPR effect of Ag NPs [30]. However, powdery photocatalysts are hard to separate from the reaction system and may therefore cause secondary pollution. Currently, no economically viable technology of spent photocatalyst separation from suspensions is available. Moreover, the abnormal light-induced charge transfer in powdery photocatalysts limits the efficiency of light energy conversion. The immobilization of the particulate photocatalyst on conducting substrate also enhances the catalytic activity by the long range charge separation [31].

Herein, we report the one-step solvothermal fabrication of Ag/CD/TNTA nanocomposites with variable Ag loadings from TNTA (prepared by electrochemical anodization), ethylene glycol (EG), and AgNO3 as raw materials (Fig. 1 ), comparing the photocatalytic performances (photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) and recyclabilities of different catalysts and analyzing the photocatalytic degradation mechanism [32].

Ti sheets were purchased from the Beijing Iron and Steel Institute. Acetone, anhydrous ethanol, AgNO3, MB, NH4F, and EG were procured from GuangFu Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

Ti sheets were cut into 2 cm × 6 cm pieces, sequentially washed with ethanol, water, and acetone, dried, sealed, and subjected to electrochemical anodization as described elsewhere to prepare TNTA [33–35]. Briefly, two Ti sheets immersed into a solution (prepared at room temperature) containing 98 vol% EG, 2 vol% H2O, and 0.3 wt% NH4F were connected to a DC power supply. The power was turned on, the voltage was adjusted to 30 V, and the solution was slowly stirred. After 1 h, the anodized Ti plate was washed with ethanol and water, heated at 2 °C min−1 to 450 °C, held at this temperature for 2 h, and cooled at the same rate to obtain anatase TNTA.

AgNO3 (10, 50, or 100 mg) was added to EG (25 mL) under vigorous stirring in the dark, and the reaction mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave along with calcined TNTA. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 5 h, and the resulting photocatalyst was washed with ethanol and water several times and dried under vacuum overnight at 40 °C. The photocatalysts obtained at AgNO3 loadings of 10, 50, and 100 mg were denoted as 1-Ag/CDs/TNTA, 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA, and 3-Ag/CDs/TNTA, respectively. For comparison, a CD/TNTA photocatalyst was prepared under identical conditions in the absence of AgNO3.

The obtained catalysts were probed by Raman microscopy (RM 2000, Renishaw, England, 514 nm), X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy (Shimadzu XRD-6000, Japan, 2° min−1), fluorescence microscopy (NIKON Ti-S, Japan; under UV light, bright field, green light, and blue light), transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM 2010FEF, Japan, 200 kV), scanning electron microscopy (SEM; LEO 1530, Germany), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy, VP microscopy (LEO, Germany, 15 kV), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; AXIS Supra, Kratos, Japan), diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus, Japan), fluorescence spectrophotometry (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan, 320 nm) and UV–vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu UV-1700, 200–700 nm).

Photocatalytic activity was defined as the ability to promote MB photodegradation upon irradiation with visible and UV light. Typically, an 8-W UV light source was placed at a distance of 10 cm from the reaction vial [30]. The reaction system was kept in the dark for 5 h to establish an adsorption-desorption equilibrium, and a 2 cm × 3 cm effective area of the photocatalyst was immersed into the MB solution (10 mL; 5 mg L−1). The extent of MB degradation was determined by UV–vis spectrophotometry.

The best-performing sample was subjected to photocatalytic activity testing, washed with ethanol and water several times, dried under vacuum overnight at 40 °C, and subjected to another activity test. This procedure was repeated multiple times to determine the effect of cycling on catalyst performance.

Compared with that of TNTA, the XRD pattern of CDs/TNTA featured the same (but less intense) peaks, which was ascribed to the presence of CDs. The peaks of Ag/CDs/TNTA at 38.2°, 44.4°, 65.1°, and 77.5° corresponded to crystal faces of standard crystalline Ag (PDF#04-0783), respectively (
), demonstrating the presence of Ag NPs. The intensity of these peaks increased with increasing AgNO3 loading, indicating a concomitant increase in the amount of Ag incorporated in the photocatalyst. Other diffraction peaks matched those of anatase TNTA, i.e., it was hard for Ag to enter the TiO2 lattice even after the high-temperature reaction.

Photocatalyst structure was further probed by inverted fluorescence microscopy. Fig. S1(a–e) represents all experimental and control groups in a bright field of view under UV, blue, and green light, revealing that light images well matched fluorescence images. TNTA showed no fluorescence when illuminated with any of the three light sources, whereas CDs/TNTA and Ag/CDs/TNTA exhibited fluorescence because of the presence of numerous well-dispersed CDs. These results confirmed the presence of CDs in CDs/TNTA and Ag/CDs/TNTA. Ag loading did not significantly affect the fluorescence of Ag/CDs/TNTA, which suggested that this fluorescence mainly originated from CDs.

Morphology and composition were further probed by SEM, TEM, and EDX analyses (Fig. 4 ). SEM imaging revealed that all samples featured nanotubes (∼50 nm in diameter and 1–2 μm long) arranged perpendicularly to the Ti sheet surface. The distribution of CDs in CDs/TNTA could not be observed because of the small CD size. According to EDX spectra, TNTA contained O (63.88 at%) and Ti (36.12 at%), and the slight deviation from the expected 2:1 stoichiometry was ascribed to the influence of the underlying Ti substrate. Apart from O (61.17 at%) and Ti (30.93 at%), the solvothermally synthesized CDs/TNTA contained C (7.90 at%), and the deviation from the 2:1 O:Ti ratio was explained as above.

In line with SEM observations, TEM imaging (Fig. 5 ) showed that CDs/TNTA had a tubular structure with a tube length of 1–2 μm, revealing that numerous CDs were observed on the nanotube surface at higher magnification. The lattice spacings of 0.237 and 0.210 nm were ascribed to the (004) plane of anatase TiO2 and the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (PDF#41-1487), respectively. Thus, these results confirmed the loading of CDs onto TNTA.

Fig. 6 shows that the ternary hybrid featured nanotubes (∼50 nm in diameter and 1–2 μm in length) aligned with the binary. CDs were not observed because of their small size. For 1-Ag/CDs/TNTA, the Ag NP content was small, and the particle size was close to 140 nm. Ag NPs were mainly deposited at the openings and tips of TiO2 nanotubes. Notably, 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA had a higher content of Ag NPs, which were widely distributed in the inner and top parts of TiO2 nanotubes and had sizes of 160 nm. Finally, 3-Ag/CDs/TNTA had an even higher content of Ag NPs, which had sizes of 260 nm and completely blocked the pores of TiO2 nanotubes. EDX spectroscopy showed that Ag/CDs/TNTA contained C, O, Ti, and Ag (Table S1).

The presence of carbon was ascribed to the incorporation of abundant CDs, and the content of Ag expectedly increased with increasing AgNO3 loading. The above results showed that the amount of Ag NPs loaded on TNTA could be regulated by adjusting the amount of AgNO3. Both the amount and size of Ag NPs increased with increasing AgNO3 amount. At low AgNO3 loadings, Ag NPs were few and small, with only a thin NP layer observed on the nozzles of TiO2 nanotubes, whereas the abundant and large NPs observed at high AgNO3 loadings blocked the nanotube nozzles.

TEM imaging of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA (Fig. 7 ) revealed that similarly to CDs/TNTA, this catalyst had an obvious nanotube structure with a tube length of 1–2 μm. The nanotube ends were decorated with Ag NPs, and hence, the catalyst had a significantly deeper color than pure TiO2 nanotubes. The lattice spacings of 0.237 and 0.350 nm were ascribed to the (004) and (101) anatase planes, respectively, whereas the spacings of 0.200 and 0.210 nm corresponded to the (200) plane of standard crystalline Ag and the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (PDF#41-1487), respectively. These results indicated that Ag/CD/TNTA composite photocatalysts featuring CDs and Ag NPs attached to various TNTA positions could be successfully prepared by a one-step solvothermal method.

Fig. 8 (a) presents an STEM image of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA, while Fig. 8(b–e) shows the EDX mappings of O, Ti, C, and Ag, respectively, and Fig. 8(f) presents the corresponding EDX spectrum. The distribution of Ti and O was consistent with the shape of TiO2 nanotubes, confirming that these nanotubes were the base material of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA. As ultra-thin carbon films were used for TEM sample preparation, a certain background distribution of carbon was observed in Fig. 8(d). However, the presence of a clear contour along the TiO2 nanotubes indicated that abundant CDs were evenly distributed on the TiO2 nanotube surface. Fig. 8(e) demonstrates that Ag was widely distributed throughout the TiO2 nanotubes. According to Fig. 8(f), no elements other than Ti, O, C, and Ag were present.

Elemental compositions and electronic states were probed by XPS. According to Fig. 9 (a), 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA contained Ag (1.82 at%), C (53.34 at%), O (34.92 at%), and Ti (9.92 at%), in line with EDX spectroscopy results. The peak fitting of each element was good, and the low content of Ag did not compromise catalytic activity. The Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 9(b)) featured Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 peaks at 366.8 and 373.4 eV, respectively, i.e., the difference in the binding energies between the two j states (△j, due to the metallic nature of Ag) equaled 6.6 eV [36]. Figs. 9(c) and (d) show O 1s and C 1s spectra, respectively. Peaks at 284.0, 285.0, and 288.1 eV were ascribed to CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively, whereas peaks at 529.2, 530.9, and 532.5 eV corresponded to Ti–O, CO, and CO bonds, respectively. The above results suggested that CDs were linked to TiO2 via CO bonds, which facilitated the transport of (photogenerated) electrons and thus benefitted photocatalytic activity [37]. Notably, CDs were attached to TiO2 nanoparticles rather than being doped into TiO2, as follows from the absence of Ti 2p and C 1s peaks corresponding to Ti–C bonds. Specifically, the Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 9e) featured a Ti 2p1/2 peak at 463.8 eV and a Ti 2p3/2 peak at 458.0 eV. The difference in the binding energies between the two j states (△j) of 5.8 eV indicated that Ti was present as Ti4+.

As a representative cationic organic dye, MB finds numerous industrial applications [38,39]. Herein, MB photodegradation was used to test the activity of the prepared photocatalysts. Fig. 10 (a) shows that after 80-min irradiation with UV light, TNTA achieved a degradation efficiency of only 70 %, whereas a higher value of 85 % was achieved by 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA under the same conditions. Fig. 10(b) shows that the peak position of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA remained unchanged at 664 nm during MB degradation. Further, the rates of MB degradation over different catalysts were determined from the first-order kinetic equation, as the reaction followed pseudo-first-order kinetics within a given concentration range (Fig. 10(c)) [40].

MB degradation over CDs/TNTA was faster than that over TNTA, possibly because of the excellent charge transferability of CDs, which allowed electrons to be transferred from TiO2 to CDs and effectively restrained the recombination of electron-hole pairs. The degradation rates of Ag/CDs/TNTA catalysts exceeded those of TNTA and CDs/TNTA, as in these ternary composites, the photogenerated electrons could be transferred between Ag NPs, CDs, and TNTA, which increased the efficiency of electron-hole separation. With increasing Ag loading, the degradation rate first increased and then decreased, being maximal for 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA. This behavior was explained by the fact that at a certain Ag loading, Ag NPs completely blocked the pores of TiO2 nanotubes and prevented light absorption, becoming the complex centers of electron-hole pair photogeneration to reduce photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 10(d), 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA maintained sufficient catalytic performance after three repeated degradation experiments.

Fig. 11 shows the MB degradation performances of various catalysts under visible light. As TiO2 (bandgap =3.2 eV) cannot effectively induce the generation of electron-hole pairs under visible light, only 10 % MB was degraded over TNTA within 4 h, whereas the corresponding value for 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA equaled 45 %. The performances of different catalysts were further determined by fitting kinetic data by a pseudo-first-order model. Fig. 11(b) shows that the MB degradation rate of CDs/TNTA exceeded that of TNTA, as CDs exhibited excellent charge transferability and could promote charge separation. The degradation rate of Ag/CDs/TNTA exceeded that of CDs/TNTA because of the effects of CD and Ag NP loadings. With increasing Ag loading, the degradation rate increased and then decreased. In particular, the degradation rate of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA was four times higher than that of TNTA. The SPR effect of Ag NPs increased the efficiency of visible light absorption and thus endowed Ag/CDs/TNTA with excellent catalytic activity under visible light irradiation. However, excessive Ag NPs blocked the pores of TiO2 nanotubes and promoted the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thus decreasing photocatalytic activity.

As shown in Fig. S2, 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA maintained catalytic activity during two repeated degradation experiments, achieving an MB degradation efficiency of 45 % within 4 h and thus exhibiting excellent recyclability. Importantly, Ag/CDs/TNTA had a higher photocatalytic degradation activity than TNTA and CDs/TNTA, regardless of whether UV or visible light was used. Moreover, the ternary composites could be recycled and reused while retaining their activity, thus effectively overcoming the disadvantages of powdery photocatalyst usage and holding great potential for the mitigation of environmental pollution.

The roles of CDs and Ag NPs in ternary catalysts were probed by diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 12 ). The strong absorption of TNTA below 400 nm was due to the transition between the inherent bands of TiO2, while light scattering due to cracks and pores accounted for visible light absorption. The intensity of the latter absorption for CDs/TNTA influenced the maximal performance of TiO2, which was mainly reflected by the fact that CDs/TNTA was a much stronger absorber than TNTA within a given wavelength range. Compared with that of CDs/TNTA, the absorption of Ag/CDs/TNTA did not significantly change in the UV region but was significantly enhanced in the visible range of 400–600 nm because of the SPR effect of Ag NPs. Among the three Ag/CDs/TNTA photocatalysts, 1-Ag/CDs/TNTA featured the weakest visible light absorption, with no absorption peak observed between 400 and 600 nm. This behavior was ascribed to the low Ag content and small NP size, which precluded detection by diffuse reflection spectroscopy. 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA and 3-Ag/CDs/TNTA showed weak broad absorption peaks at 475 nm and 430 nm, respectively, the visible absorption capacity of 3-Ag/CDs/TNTA exceeded those of 1-Ag/CDs/TNTA and 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA, which indicated that 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA and 3-Ag/CDs/TNTA had higher Ag loadings than 1-Ag/CDs/TNTA. Notably, the different samples exhibited almost identical bandgaps, which confirmed that CDs were attached to TNTA rather than doped into the nanotube interior.

Fig. S3 compares pristine and spent catalysts, revealing that upon anodization, the smooth and uniformly colored Ti sheet became darker and lost luster, which indicated the formation of TiO2 nanotubes. Calcination converted amorphous TiO2 into crystalline anatase, which resulted in a pale blue color. Finally, the deposition of Ag NPs rendered the photocatalyst surface pale yellow.

Fig. 13 shows the PL spectra of catalysts recorded in the inactive condition at a wavelength of 320 nm. All samples showed strong and broad emission peaks between 350 and 550 nm as well as a clear excitation peak at 440 nm. PL intensity, which increased in the order of 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA < 1-Ag/CDs/TNTA < 3-Ag/CDs/TNTA < CDs/TNTA < TNTA, is negatively correlated with electron-hole separation efficiency. Thus, TNTA featured the lowest electron-hole separation efficiency. The excellent charge transferability of CDs allowed them to accept photogenerated electrons transferred from TiO2, which made the PL intensity of CDs/TNTA lower than that of TNTA. Compared with that of TNTA and CDs/TNTA, the PL intensity of Ag/CDs/TNTA was significantly reduced, indicating that electrons could be transferred between CDs, Ag NPs, and TNTA to effectively inhibit electron-hole recombination. In particular, 2-Ag/CDs/TNTA had the lowest PL intensity among the three Ag/CDs/TNTA catalysts, thus featuring the highest efficiency of electron-hole separation upon excitation at 320 nm. These results suggest that initially, the recombination efficiency of electron-hole pairs decreased with increasing Ag loading, whereas at overly high Ag loadings, Ag NPs blocked the channels of TiO2 nanotubes.

Fig. 14 presents a possibly plausible photocatalytic reaction mechanism under UV and visible light. As a photosensitizer, TiO2 promotes the generation of electron-hole pairs under the action of UV light. The electrons are excited by UV light and transferred from the valence band to the conduction band, while the same number of holes is created in the valence band. With attaching Ag NPs to TiO2, a Schottky barrier is formed between Ag and TiO2 because the valence band energy of TiO2 is much higher than the Fermi energy of Ag. And the photoexcited electrons are transferred from TiO2 to Ag under the drive of this potential energy. Therefore, the recombination of electron-hole pairs is inhibited, and the lifetime of electron-hole pairs increases effectively. Moreover, the excellent charge transferability of CDs allows electrons to be transferred between CDs, Ag NPs, and TiO2, which further inhibits electron-hole pair recombination. The photoexcited electrons are captured by O2 and H2O adsorbed on the sample surface to form OH
, while the holes in the TiO2 valence band are captured by OH− to form OH
. These highly active radicals effectively mineralize pollutants to H2O and CO2.

The large bandgap of TiO2 precludes its excitation by visible light. However, the strong SPR effect of Ag NPs allows visible light to generate high-energy electrons on their surface. These electrons are then transferred to CDs, which facilitates electron-hole pair separation, and finally react with O2 and TNTA to form O2 −.
These radical anions finally degrade the pollutant into H2O and CO2. At the same time, the holes on Ag NPs move to photocatalyst and directly oxidize the organic pollutant. In conclusion, the SPR effect of Ag NPs and the excellent charge transfer ability of CDs enhance the photocatalytic performance of Ag/CDs/TNTA under visible light.

TNTA prepared by anodization were used as a raw material for the one-step solvothermal fabrication of Ag/CDs/TNTA composite photocatalysts featuring vertically arranged TiO2 nanotubes decorated with CDs and Ag NPs. CDs were linked to TiO2 through CO bonds and facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electrons. Compared to CDs/TNTA and TNTA, Ag/CDs/TNTA exhibited better performance under both visible and UV light because of the SPR effect of Ag NPs and the excellent charge transfer ability of CDs. The loading of Ag on the TNTA surface could be effectively adjusted by changing the amount of the AgNO3 precursor. When the AgNO3 dosage was low, few small Ag NPs particles were generated, and only a very thin layer of Ag NPs was present on nanotube nozzles. At an overly high dosage of AgNO3, the generated Ag NPs were too large and blocked the pore channels of TiO2 nanotubes, acting as centers for electron-hole recombination and thus reducing photocatalytic activity. Ag/CDs/TNTA maintained photocatalytic activity after several cycles and could be reused, thus holding great promise for environmental protection and wastewater treatment applications.

Xiaohui Wei: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis. Chunlai Wang: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Data curation, Formal analysis. Sheng Ding: Writing - review & editing, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Kun Yang: Writing - review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Feng Tian: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Fan Li: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author affirms that there are no competing financial interests.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jece.2020.104729.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ imaging of photocatalytic activity at titanium dioxide nanotubes using scanning ion conductance microscopy
Anal. Chem.

Acta Biomater.

J. Clean. Prod.

Appl. Surf. Sci.
